# Cross-Well Intelligence and the Industrial DDR Platform {#sec-chapter-12}

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

- Classify operational text by phase and NPT status.
- Build a co-occurrence graph of operational terms as a lightweight,
  inspectable alternative to a full knowledge graph.
- Explain how a leading-indicator causal pattern is detected across a
  phase boundary using simple, auditable thresholds — no black-box model
  required.
- See how everything from Chapters 1–11 combines into the kind of finding
  that justifies building a system like this at all.

## Operational problem

This is the payoff question every previous chapter has been building
toward: *did something in an earlier operational phase predict trouble in
a later one?* A human reading 171 PDFs in sequence, months apart, is
unlikely to connect a debris-recovery run in July to a completion-phase
overpull pattern in August — not because the connection isn't visible in
the text, but because no one reads 171 reports in one sitting while
holding all of them in mind at once. A system that has structured,
classified, and indexed the same text *can* hold all of it at once.

## Example DDR extract

::: {.callout-note title="A real cross-phase finding from the companion repository"}
> 26 magnet-BHA passes across the frac sleeve profiles during the
> production-phase debris recovery campaign correlate with overpull
> events escalating **8.4×** once the completion phase began at the same
> depths — 5 occurrences in PROD1's final fortnight became 42 in COMPZN's
> first, which also shows up month-over-month as 29 occurrences in July
> becoming 71 in August.
>
> The system's auto-generated recommendation: check sleeve condition from
> the final magnet run log *before* FIA tool selection finalises — the
> kind of handover note that usually only exists if someone happens to
> remember it three months on.

The ten days bridging the two phases (DDRs 110–119, 29 Jul–9 Aug 2024) are
entirely missing from the corpus — the system built this finding *around*
a hole in its own source record, using Chapter 10's gap-awareness to know
exactly what it didn't have.
:::

## Theory

Three components, layered:

1. **Phase classification.** Each operations line is tagged with an
   operational phase (e.g. `PROD1`, `COMPZN`) by matching op-code
   prefixes against a lookup table — a `PHASE_MAP`, not a trained
   classifier. Simple, and precisely as good as the mapping you maintain.
2. **Co-occurrence graphs.** Rather than building a full knowledge graph
   with typed relationships, track which operational terms appear
   *together* within a report or a time window, and how often. This is
   dramatically cheaper to build and easier to inspect than a knowledge
   graph, while still surfacing the kind of "these things keep showing up
   together" signal an engineer would call meaningful.
3. **Leading-indicator detection across phase boundaries.** For a term
   like "overpull," track its frequency in a window (e.g. 14 days) before
   and after each phase transition. Call it **causal** only above a
   defined post-phase NPT rate threshold (e.g. ≥40%), and **escalating**
   only above a defined frequency-increase threshold (e.g. ≥50%). These
   thresholds are plain numbers you can read, change, and justify — not
   weights inside a trained model you'd need to interpret indirectly.

The discipline that makes a finding like the one above trustworthy is
that **every number in it is re-derivable**: the 8.4× figure, the 26
passes, the 18 report dates — all computed from the same structured
`ddr_facts.parquet` and event tables the rest of this book has been
building toward, not asserted by a model. That's the difference between
"the system found a pattern" and "the system found a pattern you can
check."

## Implementation

```{python}
#| eval: false
# code/chapter_12/phase_transition_signal.py
from collections import Counter

def term_frequency_by_window(events: list[dict], term: str,
                               phase_boundary_date, window_days: int = 14):
    """Count occurrences of `term` in the N days before and after a
    phase boundary date. `events` is a list of {"date", "text"} dicts."""
    from datetime import timedelta

    before = phase_boundary_date - timedelta(days=window_days)
    after = phase_boundary_date + timedelta(days=window_days)

    before_count = sum(
        1 for e in events
        if before <= e["date"] < phase_boundary_date and term in e["text"].lower()
    )
    after_count = sum(
        1 for e in events
        if phase_boundary_date <= e["date"] < after and term in e["text"].lower()
    )
    return before_count, after_count

def is_escalating(before_count: int, after_count: int, threshold: float = 1.5) -> bool:
    if before_count == 0:
        return after_count > 0
    return (after_count / before_count) >= threshold
```

This is a minimal version of the windowing logic behind the companion
repository's `src/ddr_rag/causality_analyzer.py`, which additionally
computes post-phase NPT rate, classifies terms as "causal," and chains
multiple such findings into the leading-indicator risk scan shown on the
Cross-Phase Causality dashboard page. Phase classification itself lives
in `src/ddr_rag/npt_classifier.py` (`PHASE_MAP`), and the co-occurrence
graph construction is in `src/ddr_rag/graph_builder.py`.

## Practical exercise

**Try it yourself:** Using the five sample DDRs from Part I, tag each
report's date and phase by hand, and use `term_frequency_by_window()` to
check whether "torque" mentions increase in the days immediately before
the stuck pipe event on DDR 18.

**You'll know it worked when:** you can state, with the actual counts,
whether "torque" is escalating in the window before the stuck pipe event
— and you can point to exactly which reports you counted.

## Challenge exercise

**Challenge:** Clone the companion repository, run its full pipeline
against its sample data, and open the **Cross-Phase Causality** dashboard
page. Find one leading-indicator finding, and trace it back — using the
DDR citations the dashboard provides — to the actual report text that
produced it.

## Key takeaways

- Phase classification, co-occurrence graphs, and threshold-based
  causal detection are individually simple techniques; their combination,
  applied across a full campaign, produces findings a human reading
  linearly is unlikely to catch.
- Every threshold in a system like this should be a number you can name
  and justify, not a hyperparameter buried in a trained model.
- This is the payoff for every discipline earlier chapters insisted on:
  OCR gating so the text is trustworthy, structured chunking so it's
  retrievable, hybrid retrieval so it's findable, traceability so it's
  checkable, evaluation so you know where it's weak. None of it was
  optional — this chapter is why.

## Repository files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `code/chapter_12/phase_transition_signal.py` | Minimal windowed frequency/escalation check |
| `DDR_RAG_Pipeline/src/ddr_rag/npt_classifier.py` | Phase classification (companion repo) |
| `DDR_RAG_Pipeline/src/ddr_rag/graph_builder.py` | Co-occurrence graph construction (companion repo) |
| `DDR_RAG_Pipeline/src/ddr_rag/causality_analyzer.py` | Leading-indicator causal detection (companion repo) |

## Suggested next step

You've now built, chapter by chapter, a smaller version of the same
system running as [DDR_RAG_Pipeline](https://github.com/djimrastephane/DDR_RAG_Pipeline).
[Appendix A](../appendix/appendix_a_environment_setup.qmd) shows how to
clone and run the full platform — OCR, hybrid retrieval, 17-page
dashboard, and all — against your own DDR archive.
